text:
A flight home for Olympic gold medalist Daniel Wiffen left members of the public horrified after a flight secret was inadvertently unveiled.

The Irish athlete was seen posing wth his gold and bronze medals from this year's Olympics, held in Paris, alongside an Irish flag. But it was not the pose or positioning of the flag which turned users onto a flight hack but where the photo was taken.

Wiffen, who picked up a gold medal in the Men's 800m Freestyle Swimming event, can be seen holding his pair of medals and dangling out the front of the plane - something members of the public did not think was possible.

One user wrote: "I didn't know those windows opened and now I have a new fear.

"Fair play to Daniel and all, I would have just been more comfortable not knowing you could slide back to cockpit windows like they're on an old Nissan Micra."

Read more:Imane Khelif 'names Elon Musk and JK Rowling in lawsuit' after boxing gender row

Members of the public have since tried to calm the worried flyer and say it takes a little more than a roll up handle to open and close the cockpit windows. One Twitter/X user replied: "From memory it takes a bit of work to open them. You’d sometimes see them open when plane parked for pilot to talk with ground crew. Nothing to fear nowadays."

Another reassured the original poster, adding: "They're very hard to open on the Airbus so if you fly Aer Lingus you're fine."

Don't miss:Olympic star Daniel Wiffen rushed to hospital after missing closing ceremony

Don't miss:Wiffen has big plans for final Olympics weekend after bagging Ireland two medals

Don't miss:NASCAR star explains belief he's superior driver to F1 champion Max Verstappen

But other users recalled an infamous incident where a pilot was sucked out the front of the plane window. British Airways Flight 5390 is remembered for its explosive decompression, where captain Timothy Lancaster, 42 at the time, was pulled from the cockpit.

We use your sign-up to provide content in ways you've consented to and to improve our understanding of you. This may include adverts from us and 3rd parties based on our understanding. You can unsubscribe at any time. Read ourPrivacy Policy

Flight attendant Nigel Ogden and later Chief Steward John Heward and flight attendant Simon Rogers kept Lancaster from leaving the cockpit entirely, holding onto his legs as they made an emergency landing at Southampton Airport.

One user wrote: "Sometimes (once) they use the wrong size bolts and the pilot gets sucked out. Flight BA 5390. The pilot was grabbed by the crew and survived." Another added that the original, nervous user should not "look up British Airways Flight 111".

title: Olympic gold medallist's flight home unlocks 'new fear' | UK | News | Express.co.uk
author: Ewan Gleadow
description: A flight home for Olympic gold medallist Daniel Wiffen horrified members of the public who only just realised something about plane windows.
